design_philosophy.core_loop: Grid-based tactical positioning and turn-order management with resource depletion (fatigue) forcing strategic unit rotation and rest decisions
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing limited unit availability through fatigue system while completing map objectives with varying requirements (kill targets, rescue allies, escape unharmed)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a rebel army to liberate an oppressed kingdom by making tactical positioning decisions and recovering from tactical setbacks
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Fatigue system as core mechanic—punishes excessive unit action by benching characters next chapter, forcing genuine squad depth and preventing dominant-unit strategies. Variability in stat growth and multiple mission objective types (not just 'defeat all') create unpredictable challenges. Fog of War on certain maps increases information asymmetry. Class evolution is predetermined (not player-chosen), reinforcing narrative progression over build customization.

difficulty.checkpointing: Per-map checkpointing (missions are discrete chapters; failure requires restart)
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited. Player cannot adjust difficulty setting. Fatigue system creates implicit difficulty curve—early game has smaller rosters, later chapters allow unit cycling. Random stat growth on level-up means same map can vary in difficulty across playthroughs. Mission objective variety (escape vs. eliminate) forces tactical adaptation rather than formulaic approaches.

progression.upgrade_system: Free weapon swapping within character class constraints; mounted units swap between lances/axes (mounted) and swords (dismounted) with stat penalties when dismounted. Stat growth via experience points and leveling with random stat increments (capped at 20 per stat). Class evolution is story-driven and automatic.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Class evolution tied to narrative progression. Unit roster expands through story events (rescuing allies like Nanna and Mareeta). After mid-game, advance unit selection becomes available, allowing pre-battle composition planning.
replayability_hooks: Random stat growth creates different power curves per playthrough
Fatigue management creates varied squad rotation strategies
Multiple map objectives types (kill targets, rescue, escape) require different tactical approaches
Weapon Triangle system (sword>axe>lance>sword) encourages counter-positioning decisions

world_building_techniques: Mission-specific objectives embedded in narrative context (rescuing kidnapped allies, reclaiming homeland)
Contextual map features (towns, churches grant currency/health, suggesting civilian infrastructure)
Character recruitment through story events (expanding roster reflects alliance-building)

boss_design.philosophy: Multi-phase boss encounters with narrative significance. Raydrik serves as recurring antagonist with escalating threat level; final form transforms into Deadlord Mus (undead monster) after defeat, suggesting magical corruption. Veld (high-ranking priest) represents ideological opposition and arcane threat.
notable_bosses: Raydrik (Duke of Munster)—recurring boss, transforms into Deadlord Mus upon defeat
Veld (Loptous Sect priest)—ideological antagonist, transforms enemy upon magical power

faction_indicators: Loptous Sect (religious order opposed to protagonist)
Republic of Munster (protagonist's homeland, requires liberation)
Fiana village (civilian refuge, destroyed to motivate protagonist)
danger_signals: Raydrik's attack on Fiana village and kidnapping of civilians (Nanna, Mareeta) signals escalating military oppression
Transformation of Raydrik into undead Deadlord suggests dark magical practices by ruling powers
Fog of War mechanic on certain maps simulates information control and enemy concealment
Forced unit fatigue creates supply-line vulnerability—army exhaustion as military weakness
environmental_storytelling: Towns and churches as civilian infrastructure (suggest occupied territories)
Varied terrain (mountains, plains) as geographic barriers defending/limiting expansion
Dismounting mechanics in buildings reflect practical occupation of civilian spaces
